title: The 10 Actors Who Won 2022. Plus: ‘Bardo’!
format: Sean and Amanda run through the year's best performances, actor by actor, then Adam Nayman joins to fight about Alejandro González Iñárritu's Bardo.

films.title: Top Gun Maverick
films.note: Tom Cruise, whose real-life skydive test videos got nearly as much airtime this week as the movie. Miles Teller plays Goose's son more restrained than either host expected; Glenn Powell is pure movie-star id as Hangman; Jennifer Connelly gets the thankless love-interest role and makes every scene of it work anyway.

films.title: TÁR
films.note: Cate Blanchett as conductor Lydia Tár, the performance the whole movie is built around, down to the custom suiting.

films.title: Everything Everywhere All at Once
films.note: Michelle Yeoh holds a multiverse movie together on pure range: martial artist, laundromat owner, mother, sci-fi lead, and genuinely funny throughout.

films.title: Elvis
films.note: Austin Butler's first performance to really register beyond a small Once Upon a Time in Hollywood supporting turn: he has charisma and presence past the impersonator cliché. Sean liked the film less than Amanda did, mostly over its chopped-up flashback structure.

films.title: After Yang
films.note: Colin Farrell, quiet and restrained, in Kogonada's family sci-fi, the first of three performances from him this year.

films.title: The Batman
films.note: Farrell under latex as the Penguin, having a lot of fun hamming it up.

films.title: The Banshees of Inisherin
films.note: Farrell's best of the three: a simple, charming man trying to understand why his best friend has abandoned him, a long way from the guy who played Bullseye in 2006's Daredevil.

films.title: Nope
films.note: Keke Palmer, praised in brief.

films.title: X
films.note: Mia Goth in dual roles for Ti West's slasher.

films.title: Pearl
films.note: Goth's origin story for one of her X characters, built around a six-minute monologue the camera just holds on her face.

films.title: The Menu
films.note: Hong Chau elevates a chef's-table satire the moment she starts herding the boat group off the dock.

films.title: The Whale
films.note: Chau finding layers in an underwritten role, meeting Brendan Fraser where he is.

films.title: Emily the Criminal
films.note: Aubrey Plaza doing character work: an accent, no makeup, a stripped-down performance far from the sarcastic Parks and Rec register she's known for.

films.title: Triangle of Sadness
films.note: Dolly De Leon steals the film's third act; a likely Best Supporting Actress nominee if the film gets a Best Picture nod.

films.title: Armageddon Time
films.note: Anthony Hopkins as James Gray's grandfather stand-in, maybe the film where he's most clearly playing himself, gravitas and all.

films.title: Barbarian
films.note: Justin Long's entrance is the hardest either host laughed all year, in a movie that hadn't been comic up to that point.

films.title: Causeway
films.note: Brian Tyree Henry, in what plays as a two-hander with Jennifer Lawrence.

films.title: The Woman King
films.note: Lashana Lynch and Thuso Mbedu, both terrific; Mbedu in particular takes the movie over in its back half.

films.title: Ambulance
films.note: Jake Gyllenhaal, turned up to 13 opposite Michael Bay: not a good performance, but a fun one.

films.title: Decision to Leave
films.note: Tang Wei, singled out as exceptional.

films.title: Aftersun
films.note: Paul Mescal and his young co-star Frankie Corio, both wonderful.

films.title: Bardo, False Chronicle of a Handful of Truths
films.note: Critic Adam Nayman joins to argue about Alejandro González Iñárritu's quasi-autobiographical film about a documentarian caught between Mexico and Hollywood. Amanda watched the shorter theatrical cut and slept through parts of it; Sean saw the original three-hour version at Telluride before Iñárritu trimmed 15 to 20 minutes. Nayman, who has written at length about Iñárritu, calls the film a show of technical power in service of not much: self-aggrandizement over the sincere material (a dead child, a fractured national identity) buried inside it. All three agree the film's ocean sequence, dealing with the loss of a child, genuinely got to them, even as they otherwise found the movie insufferable.

referenced.group: Iñárritu's back catalog, discussed at length
